group.title: Acceptance and sign-off of the Platform

proposition_text: Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
summary: The pleaded allegation is absolute: that Meridian accepted no part of the Platform and gave no acceptance or sign-off at any time. That categorical assertion is directly refuted by the contemporaneous documentary record and by Meridian's own witness. On 12 November 2024 Meridian, through its Head of Procurement Priya Nair, signed a Phase 1 UAT Acceptance Certificate (doc_008) expressly confirming that Phase 1 of the Platform had passed user acceptance testing and 'is accepted', subject only to minor Severity 3/4 snags. Priya Nair confirms in her own witness statement (doc_018) that she signed that certificate, and TechFlow's pre-action response (doc_015) relies on it. The allegation is the pleaded case at paragraph 13 of the Particulars of Claim (doc_002), but the blanket wording ('any part', 'at any time') is falsified because at least one part (Phase 1) was formally accepted and signed off. The proposition is therefore adverse. Confidence is high but not absolute because the acceptance was expressly limited to Phase 1 and subject to outstanding snags, so it does not defeat the wider quality case; nevertheless the proposition's categorical denial of any acceptance whatsoever is squarely contradicted.

evidence.reasoning: The Claim Form gives the brief details of the claim (breach, defective/delayed delivery) but does not address acceptance or sign-off of the Platform, so it does not bear on this allegation.

This is the source pleading from which the allegation is drawn (paragraph 13 of the Particulars of Claim). It asserts the proposition verbatim as Meridian's pleaded case, and so supports the allegation as a matter of pleading. Tested against the rest of the bundle, however, the categorical pleading is undermined: it is contradicted by the signed Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate (doc_008), by TechFlow's reliance on that certificate (doc_015) and by Meridian's own witness Priya Nair (doc_018). It is therefore the assertion of the allegation rather than independent proof of it.
evidence.excerpt: 13.Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.

evidence.reasoning: The Master Services Agreement defines the Platform and provides the contractual framework (scope, change control, limitation), but it does not itself record any acceptance or sign-off event, nor does it address whether Meridian accepted the Platform. It is background to the allegation rather than evidence for or against it.
evidence.excerpt: 1.1  The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").

evidence.reasoning: The Statement of Work sets out the two-phase scope and a User Acceptance Testing milestone (M3), which is relevant context for what acceptance/sign-off would relate to, but it records no actual acceptance and does not bear directly on whether Meridian in fact accepted the Platform.
The Order Form records the commercial line items and authorising signatures at contract formation. It does not address acceptance or sign-off of the delivered Platform.
The Deed of Variation No. 1 only re-profiles the payment milestones and expressly does not vary scope or go-live date. It says nothing about acceptance or sign-off of the Platform.
Change Order No. 3 adds the loyalty module and revises the go-live date. It concerns scope and timetable, not acceptance or sign-off of the Platform, so it does not address the allegation.

This is the central document on the allegation and directly contradicts it. The Phase 1 UAT Acceptance Certificate, signed for Meridian by Priya Nair on 12 November 2024, expressly states that Phase 1 of the Platform passed UAT and 'is accepted', subject only to minor Severity 3/4 snags. This shows that Meridian did accept at least one part of the Platform and did give a written sign-off, falsifying the absolute claim that no acceptance or sign-off was given 'at any time' and that Meridian accepted no part of the Platform.
evidence.excerpt:
Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted, subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor (Severity 3 and 4) items listed in the snag log appended to the UAT report. No outstanding Severity 1 or Severity 2 items were recorded at the date of this certificate.

Accepted for Meridian

....................................
Priya Nair
Head of Procurement
12 November 2024

evidence.reasoning: This go-live decision email shows Meridian instructing TechFlow to proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 despite TechFlow's recommendation to defer. While a go-live instruction is loosely related to taking the Platform into use, it is not an acceptance or sign-off of the Platform's quality and does not directly speak to formal acceptance; it is more probative of the go-live timing dispute than of the acceptance allegation.
evidence.excerpt: Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side.

evidence.reasoning: The loyalty module change-request email concerns scope and the revised go-live date. It does not address acceptance or sign-off of the Platform.

This email concerns the root cause of the 25 November outage (network failure, not the Platform). It is relevant to availability/causation, not to whether Meridian accepted or signed off the Platform.
This internal Meridian email addresses the causes of poor December trading (DC flood, weak market, stock-sync issues). It does not address acceptance or sign-off of the Platform.
The defect/issue log records post-go-live defects and their causes. It is relevant to quality and availability but does not address whether Meridian accepted or signed off the Platform.

Meridian's termination/letter before action asserts repudiatory breach and that the Platform was not of satisfactory quality, and Meridian accepts the conduct as terminating the MSA. This is consistent with Meridian's position that it ultimately rejected the Platform, but it does not state that no acceptance or sign-off was ever given, and it does not address (still less deny) the Phase 1 acceptance certificate (doc_008). It is broadly consistent with Meridian's narrative but does not establish the categorical allegation.
evidence.excerpt: Our client accepts your conduct as terminating the MSA with immediate effect and reserves all of its rights.

evidence.reasoning: TechFlow's response to the letter before action expressly relies on the signed Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate to rebut Meridian. This corroborates doc_008 and directly contradicts the allegation that no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time, confirming the existence and significance of a signed acceptance of Phase 1.
evidence.excerpt: Acceptance. Your client accepted Phase 1 by a signed Acceptance Certificate dated 12 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: Helena Vance's witness statement explains why Meridian ended the relationship ('not what we had paid for') but does not address formal acceptance or sign-off, and does not deny the Phase 1 acceptance certificate (doc_008). It supports Meridian's dissatisfaction narrative but is neutral on the specific allegation about acceptance/sign-off.
evidence.excerpt: I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

evidence.reasoning: Raymond Okafor's statement addresses store operations, the causes of till outages and the poor December trading. It does not address acceptance or sign-off of the Platform.

Priya Nair, Meridian's own Head of Procurement and a Claimant witness, confirms that she signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate on 12 November 2024 confirming that Phase 1 had passed UAT with no critical items outstanding. This is direct admission evidence from Meridian itself that an acceptance/sign-off of part of the Platform was given, squarely contradicting the absolute allegation and corroborating doc_008.
evidence.excerpt: On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.

evidence.reasoning: Dr Whitfield's IT expert report addresses Platform availability and defects. It does not address whether Meridian accepted or signed off the Platform, so it is not relevant to this allegation.
Greenhalgh's quantum report addresses wasted expenditure and loss of profit. It does not address acceptance or sign-off of the Platform.
